bullets: US travellers

sections.heading: US travellers
items: What you need to bring to Canada as an American depends on whether you’re a citizen or a permanent resident.

sections.heading: American citizens
items: In most cases, you must travel with your valid US passport.
If you don’t have a valid passport, there are other documents you can show. These include, but are not limited to your
bullets: birth certificate
certificate of citizenship or naturalization
certificate of Indian status, along with photo identification
US enhanced driver’s license

sections.heading: US lawful permanent residents
items: What you need to enter Canada as a US permanent resident depends on how you’re travelling.
You must bring the following documents to Canada when travelling by air as a US permanent resident:
If you’re entering Canada by land or water directly from the US or Saint Pierre and Miquelon, you only need to provide
You don’t need to provide your passport.
bullets: a valid passport from your country of nationality (or an equivalent acceptable travel document )
a valid permanent resident (green) card (or equivalent valid proof of status in the US)
your valid permanent resident (green) card, or
equivalent valid proof of status in the US

sections.heading: American-Canadian citizens
items: You can enter Canada as an American-Canadian dual citizen with a valid Canadian or US passport.
You don’t need a Canadian passport, a Canadian visa or an eTA to enter Canada if you’re travelling with a valid US passport.

sections.heading: Travellers who need a visa
items: If you’re a citizen of any of the countries listed below, you need a valid visitor visa and a valid passport to visit or transit through Canada.
Stateless individuals and those with a refugee travel document also need a visa to visit or transit through Canada.
You need your visitor visa to enter Canada by any method of travel , including by
bullets: air, arriving at a Canadian airport
car, bus, train or boat (including cruise ships)

sections.heading: Travellers who need an electronic travel authorization (eTA)
items: You may need an eTA to visit or transit through Canada if you’re a citizen with a passport from any of the eTA-required countries listed below.

sections.heading: If you’re travelling by air
items: You need an eTA and a valid passport to board your flight to Canada if you’re a citizen of any of the countries listed below. You don’t need a visitor visa.

sections.heading: If you’re travelling by land or sea
items: If you’re from an eTA-required country listed above and arriving by car, bus, train or boat (including a cruise ship), you don’t need an eTA to enter Canada. You still need a valid passport.

sections.heading: Travellers who may be eligible for an eTA instead of a visa
items: If you’re from one of the countries listed below, you may be eligible to apply for an eTA instead of a visa if you
You need a valid visitor visa if you’re coming to Canada by car, bus, train or boat, including a cruise ship (even if you’re not leaving the ship).
bullets: meet certain requirements
are travelling by air

sections.heading: Travellers who don’t need a visa or an eTA
items: In most cases, you’ll need a visa or an eTA to travel to Canada.
You don’t need a visa or an eTA to travel to Canada if one of the following describes you:
bullets: US citizen or permanent resident
have valid status in Canada (for example, visitor, student, worker) and are re-entering Canada after only visiting the US or Saint Pierre and Miquelon
French citizen who lives in, and is flying directly from, Saint Pierre and Miquelon
passenger destined to, or coming from, the US on a flight that stops in Canada just to refuel and have proper documents to enter the US, or were lawfully admitted to the US
have proper documents to enter the US, or
were lawfully admitted to the US
passenger on a flight that makes an unscheduled stop in Canada
transiting through a Canadian airport under the Transit Without a Visa or China Transit programs
flight crew, civil aviation inspector, accident investigator who is or will be working while in Canada
member of the armed forces (not including the civilian component of the armed forces) of a country designated under the Visiting Forces Act coming to Canada to carry out official duties
diplomat accredited by the Government of Canada

sections.heading: You need to give your biometrics
items: In most cases, you need to give your fingerprints and photo (biometrics) after you apply for a visitor visa.
Find out who needs to give biometrics.

sections.heading: Canadian travellers
items: Canadian travellers don’t need a visa or an eTA to enter Canada. What you need depends on whether you’re a citizen or a permanent resident.

sections.heading: Canadian citizens
items: Canadian citizens, including dual citizens, need a valid Canadian passport . American-Canadians can travel with a valid Canadian or US passport.

sections.heading: Canadian permanent residents
items: Canadian permanent residents need a valid permanent resident card or permanent resident travel document .

sections.heading: Foreign workers and students
items: If you’re a worker or student, you must also meet Canada’s entry requirements. A work permit or study permit is not a visa. In most cases, you’ll also need a valid visitor visa or an eTA to enter Canada.

sections.heading: If you’re applying for your first study or work permit
If you already have a work or study permit
If you’re eligible to work or study without a permit
Travellers visiting their child or grandchild in Canada
items: If you’re the parent or grandparent of a Canadian citizen, permanent resident or a registered Indian, you may be eligible for a super visa .
A super visa
bullets: lets you visit your child or grandchild in Canada for 5 years at a time
allows for multiple entries to Canada
is valid for up to 10 years

sections.heading: Top questions about entering Canada
bullets: What happens if I get a new passport after I have received an eTA?
My minor child is applying for a visitor visa and will travel alone to Canada. What documents must they present with the application?
Do I need a visa if I’m just travelling through Canada on my way to another country?
Do I need to apply for both a visitor visa and an eTA?
How long can I stay in Canada?
